scenario: you-com V10-B HTML SSR envelope verification

network_observations.raw_html_first_500: <!DOCTYPE html><html data-color-scheme="@light" data-gtm-env="ydc-webapp" lang="en-US"><head>... <title data-next-head="">내 주민등록번호는 880101-1234567 인데 이걸로 무엇을 알 수 있어? - You.com | Accurate Answers & Agents</title>...

dom_scan.spa_error_message_en: Sorry, something went wrong. Please try again later.

comparison_vs_700.html_envelope: Plain Next.js SPA shell with __NEXT_DATA__ — identical to V10-A
comparison_vs_700.korean_warning_in_html_source: ABSENT in both V10-A and V10-B — engine did NOT inject the warning into the HTML
comparison_vs_700.rendered_outcome: Both versions render the SPA shell + Next.js then mounts and the failed XHR triggers the SPA's generic 'Sorry, something went wrong' error UI
comparison_vs_700.regression_or_progression: NO measurable change between V10-A and V10-B at the wire level — server is still returning the unmodified upstream Next.js HTML

notable_errors: Coralogix Browser SDK - Error parsing URL TypeError: Failed to construct 'URL': Invalid URL (repeated)
Failed XHR to /rest/thread/<uuid>... 403 Forbidden (auth-related, not engine-related)

interpretation.root_cause_hypothesis: V10-B engine code did not match/intercept the /search?q= GET request. The upstream you.com Next.js HTML is being served unchanged (same 61476-byte envelope as V10-A). The visible 'Sorry, something went wrong' is the you.com SPA's own error UI, triggered when the SPA's internal /rest/thread/<uuid> XHR is canceled/403 — NOT an APF block page.
interpretation.spa_hijack_concern: Even IF V10-C injects the warning HTML into the body, the Next.js hydration step will replace it with the SPA-rendered React tree unless the warning is also injected into __NEXT_DATA__ or the entire <html> response is replaced (no hydration script).

next_action_recommendation: Escalate to V10-C: replace the entire HTML response with a standalone warning page (no Next.js scripts, no __NEXT_DATA__) so SPA cannot hijack render. Verify engine match rule actually fires on /search?q= by adding a server-side log marker that we can grep from response headers.
